problems.problem: (a) Name the four main types of pathogen that cause infectious disease in humans. (2 marks) (b) For each type, give one example of a disease it causes. (2 marks)

step_by_step.step: Four main types of pathogen (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: The four main types of pathogen that cause infectious disease in humans are: (1) Bacteria—single-celled prokaryotic organisms that can reproduce independently and produce toxins that damage host tissues. (2) Viruses—non-living infectious agents consisting of genetic material (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat; they require host cells to replicate. (3) Fungi—eukaryotic organisms that include yeasts and molds; they can cause superficial infections (skin, nails) or systemic infections in immunocompromised individuals. (4) Protozoa—single-celled eukaryotic organisms that are often parasitic; they are larger than bacteria and have complex life cycles often involving vectors.

Examples of diseases caused by each pathogen type: Bacteria: Tuberculosis (caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis), Streptococcus throat (Streptococcus pyogenes), or cholera (Vibrio cholerae). Viruses: Influenza (influenza virus), COVID-19 (SARS-CoV-2), measles (measles virus), or HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). Fungi: Athlete's foot (Tinea pedis), ringworm (Tinea corporis), or thrush (Candida albicans). Protozoa: Malaria (Plasmodium species), amoebic dysentery (Entamoeba histolytica), or sleeping sickness (Trypanosoma brucei).

problems.final_answer: (a) Bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protozoa. (b) Bacteria: tuberculosis; viruses: influenza; fungi: athlete's foot; protozoa: malaria. (Accept other valid examples.)

problems.problem: Explain how infectious diseases can be transmitted through: (a) Direct contact (2 marks) (b) Indirect contact (2 marks) (c) Vectors (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Direct contact transmission occurs when pathogens are transferred from an infected person to a susceptible person through physical contact without an intermediate object. This includes: (1) Person-to-person contact: touching, kissing, sexual contact, or droplet transmission from coughing or sneezing directly onto another person (e.g., influenza, COVID-19, sexually transmitted infections). (2) Animal-to-person contact: bites or scratches from infected animals (e.g., rabies from dog bites). (3) Mother-to-child: transmission during pregnancy (transplacental), childbirth (through birth canal), or breastfeeding (e.g., HIV, hepatitis B).

Indirect contact transmission occurs when pathogens are transferred from an infected person to a susceptible person via an intermediate contaminated object or surface. This includes: (1) Fomites: inanimate objects such as doorknobs, utensils, towels, bedding, medical equipment, or toys that become contaminated with pathogens from an infected person (e.g., norovirus on surfaces, staphylococcal infections from contaminated equipment). (2) Airborne transmission: pathogens can remain suspended in the air for extended periods on dust particles or aerosol droplets (e.g., tuberculosis bacteria in air droplets, chickenpox virus). (3) Contaminated food or water: ingestion of pathogens from improperly handled food or untreated water (e.g., salmonella from contaminated eggs, cholera from contaminated water).

Vector transmission involves a living organism (usually an arthropod) that carries the pathogen from one host to another without becoming ill itself. Vectors can be: (1) Biological vectors: the pathogen undergoes part of its life cycle inside the vector (e.g., mosquitoes transmitting malaria parasites, ticks transmitting Lyme disease). (2) Mechanical vectors: the vector physically carries the pathogen on its body without the pathogen replicating inside it (e.g., flies carrying bacteria from feces to food). Common disease vectors include mosquitoes (malaria, dengue, Zika), ticks (Lyme disease, Rocky Mountain spotted fever), fleas (plague), and rats (leptospirosis).

problems.final_answer: (a) Direct contact: physical contact between infected and susceptible person (touch, droplets, sexual contact). (b) Indirect contact: contact with contaminated objects (fomites), airborne droplets, or contaminated food/water. (c) Vectors: living organisms (e.g., mosquitoes, ticks) that transmit pathogens between hosts.

problems.problem: The skin and mucous membranes are the body's first line of defence. (a) Describe how the skin acts as a barrier to pathogens. (2 marks) (b) Explain how mucous membranes protect the body from infection. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: The skin provides multiple barrier functions against pathogens: (1) Physical barrier: The outer layer of the skin, the stratum corneum, consists of dead, keratinized cells that are tightly packed and continuously shed. This makes it difficult for pathogens to penetrate. Keratin is a tough, insoluble protein that provides structural integrity and resistance to microbial enzymes. (2) Chemical barrier: Sweat and sebum (produced by sebaceous glands) create an acidic environment (pH 4-6) that inhibits bacterial growth. Sebum contains fatty acids and antimicrobial peptides (such as defensins) that are toxic to many bacteria and fungi. (3) Microbial competition: The skin hosts a normal microbiome of commensal bacteria that outcompete pathogens for resources and space, a concept known as colonization resistance.

Mucous membranes line body cavities that are exposed to the external environment, including the respiratory tract, digestive tract, reproductive tract, and eyes. They protect through: (1) Physical barrier: A continuous layer of epithelial cells that are tightly joined, preventing pathogen entry. (2) Mucus production: Specialized goblet cells secrete mucus—a viscous, sticky fluid composed primarily of mucin glycoproteins. Mucus traps pathogens, dust, and debris, preventing them from reaching underlying epithelial cells. (3) Ciliary clearance: In the respiratory tract, ciliated epithelial cells beat in coordinated waves to move the mucus (with trapped pathogens) upward toward the throat, where it is swallowed or expectorated. This is often called the mucociliary escalator. (4) Antimicrobial components: Mucus contains antimicrobial substances such as lysozyme (which breaks down bacterial cell walls), lactoferrin (which binds iron needed by bacteria), and secretory IgA antibodies. In the digestive tract, stomach acid (pH 1-2) destroys most ingested pathogens.

problems.final_answer: (a) The skin acts as a physical barrier (keratinized, shedding dead cells) and a chemical barrier (acidic sweat and sebum with antimicrobial properties). (b) Mucous membranes produce sticky mucus that traps pathogens; cilia sweep mucus away; mucus contains antimicrobial enzymes like lysozyme.

problems.problem: (a) Name the type of white blood cell that carries out phagocytosis. (1 mark) (b) Describe the process of phagocytosis. (3 marks) (c) Explain why phagocytes are described as part of the non-specific immune response. (1 mark)

step_by_step.step: Type of white blood cell (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: The white blood cells that carry out phagocytosis are called phagocytes. The main types of phagocytes include neutrophils (the most abundant, first responders to infection) and macrophages (larger, longer-lived cells that also present antigens to lymphocytes). Monocytes in the blood mature into macrophages when they enter tissues. Other phagocytes include dendritic cells and eosinophils.

Phagocytosis is a multi-step process: (1) Chemotaxis and recognition: Phagocytes are attracted to the site of infection by chemical signals (chemokines) released by pathogens, damaged cells, or other immune cells. The phagocyte recognizes the pathogen through pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) that bind to pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) on the pathogen surface (e.g., lipopolysaccharide on bacteria). (2) Adherence and engulfment: The phagocyte extends pseudopodia (false feet) around the pathogen, gradually surrounding it. The pathogen is internalized into a membrane-bound vesicle called a phagosome. (3) Phagosome-lysosome fusion: The phagosome fuses with lysosomes (organelles containing digestive enzymes and antimicrobial substances) to form a phagolysosome. (4) Destruction: Inside the phagolysosome, the pathogen is destroyed by multiple mechanisms: low pH (acidic environment), digestive enzymes (lysozyme, proteases), reactive oxygen species (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide), and antimicrobial peptides. (5) Exocytosis: Indigestible debris is expelled from the phagocyte by exocytosis. In macrophages, some pathogen fragments (antigens) are presented on the cell surface to activate other immune cells.

Phagocytes are described as part of the non-specific (innate) immune response because they respond to pathogens in a general, non-selective manner. They do not distinguish between different types of pathogens—they will phagocytose any foreign particle that they recognize as non-self, regardless of its specific identity. They do not have immunological memory; their response is the same upon first exposure and subsequent exposures. In contrast, the specific (adaptive) immune response involves lymphocytes that recognize specific antigens and develop memory for future encounters. Phagocytes provide immediate but non-specific protection, acting as the first line of cellular defense.

problems.final_answer: (a) Phagocytes (neutrophils and macrophages). (b) Phagocytes recognize and attach to pathogens, engulf them by extending pseudopodia, internalize them into a phagosome, fuse with lysosomes to destroy the pathogen with enzymes and acids, and expel debris. (c) Phagocytes are non-specific because they respond to any pathogen they encounter, without distinguishing between specific types, and lack immunological memory.

problems.problem: Explain the role of lymphocytes in the specific immune response. Include in your answer: (a) How B-lymphocytes respond to infection (3 marks) (b) How T-lymphocytes respond to infection (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: B-lymphocytes (B cells) are responsible for the humoral (antibody-mediated) immune response. Their response to infection occurs as follows: (1) Activation: B cells have membrane-bound antibodies (B cell receptors) that bind to specific antigens on pathogens or free antigens. When a B cell encounters its specific antigen, it internalizes and processes it, presenting antigen fragments on its surface via MHC class II molecules. Helper T cells (specifically Tₕ cells) recognize this presentation and release cytokines that activate the B cell. (2) Clonal expansion: The activated B cell undergoes rapid mitotic division, producing a large population of identical cells (clones). (3) Differentiation: Most of these clones differentiate into plasma cells—antibody factories that produce and secrete large quantities of antibodies (up to 10,000 antibodies per second). These antibodies circulate in blood and lymph, binding to pathogens to neutralize them, opsonize them (mark for phagocytosis), or activate complement. (4) Memory cells: Some of the clones differentiate into long-lived memory B cells that persist in the body for years or decades. If the same pathogen is encountered again, memory B cells can rapidly differentiate into plasma cells, producing a faster and stronger secondary response.

T-lymphocytes (T cells) are responsible for the cell-mediated immune response and are divided into several subtypes: (1) Helper T cells (Tₕ cells, CD4⁺): These cells do not kill pathogens directly but orchestrate the immune response. They recognize antigen fragments presented on MHC class II molecules (found on antigen-presenting cells like macrophages, dendritic cells, and B cells). Upon activation, they release cytokines that: activate B cells (promoting antibody production), activate cytotoxic T cells, recruit phagocytes, and enhance the overall immune response. Helper T cells are essential for both humoral and cell-mediated immunity—they are the 'master regulators' of the adaptive immune system. (2) Cytotoxic T cells (T₍c₎ cells, CD8⁺): These are the 'killer' T cells that directly destroy infected cells. They recognize antigen fragments presented on MHC class I molecules (found on all nucleated cells). When a cell is infected with a virus (or has intracellular bacteria), it displays viral peptides on its surface via MHC class I. Cytotoxic T cells bind to these, release cytotoxic granules (perforin and granzymes) that create pores in the target cell's membrane and induce apoptosis (programmed cell death). This eliminates the infected cell and prevents the virus from replicating and spreading. (3) Regulatory T cells (Tᵣₑg cells): These suppress immune responses to prevent autoimmunity and terminate immune responses after the infection is cleared.

problems.final_answer: (a) B-lymphocytes: upon antigen encounter, they are activated by helper T cells, proliferate, and differentiate into plasma cells that secrete antibodies to neutralize pathogens, and memory B cells that provide long-term immunity. (b) T-lymphocytes: Helper T cells activate other immune cells (B cells, cytotoxic T cells) by releasing cytokines; cytotoxic T cells directly kill infected cells by releasing perforin and granzymes to induce apoptosis.

problems.problem: (a) Describe the structure of an antibody. (2 marks) (b) Explain how antibodies destroy pathogens. (2 marks) (c) State why antibodies are described as 'specific.' (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Antibodies (also called immunoglobulins, Ig) are Y-shaped glycoproteins composed of four polypeptide chains: two identical heavy chains and two identical light chains, linked by disulfide bonds. The structure consists of: (1) Variable (V) regions: Located at the tips of the Y arms (the antigen-binding fragments, Fab), these regions have highly variable amino acid sequences that form the antigen-binding site. The shape of the variable region is complementary to a specific epitope (part of an antigen), allowing precise binding. Each antibody has two identical antigen-binding sites. (2) Constant (C) regions: The stem of the Y (the Fc region, or crystallizable fragment) has a constant amino acid sequence that determines the antibody class (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE, IgD) and interacts with other immune components such as phagocytes, complement proteins, and mast cells. The hinge region between the Fab and Fc portions allows flexibility, enabling the antibody to bind to antigens at various angles.

Antibodies themselves do not directly kill pathogens; they mark them for destruction or neutralize them through several mechanisms: (1) Neutralization: Antibodies bind to toxins or viral surface proteins, blocking their ability to bind to host cell receptors. This prevents viruses from entering cells and neutralizes bacterial toxins (e.g., tetanus toxin). (2) Opsonization: Antibodies coat the surface of pathogens, with their Fc region serving as a binding site for phagocytes (which have Fc receptors). This 'tags' the pathogen for enhanced phagocytosis, significantly increasing the efficiency of engulfment. (3) Complement activation: Antibodies (particularly IgM and IgG) can trigger the classical complement pathway. Complement proteins cascade, leading to: opsonization (C3b deposition), chemotaxis (attracting phagocytes), formation of the membrane attack complex (MAC) that creates pores in bacterial membranes, causing lysis. (4) Agglutination: Antibodies can bind to multiple pathogens simultaneously, causing them to clump together. This reduces their ability to spread and makes them easier targets for phagocytes. (5) Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC): Natural killer (NK) cells recognize antibody-coated target cells and release cytotoxic granules to kill them, particularly useful against virus-infected cells and parasites.

Antibodies are described as 'specific' because the variable region of each antibody has a unique three-dimensional shape that is complementary to a specific epitope (a particular molecular structure) on an antigen. This specificity arises from the highly diverse gene rearrangements that occur during B cell development, generating billions of different antibody specificities. An antibody that binds to an epitope on the influenza virus will not bind to an epitope on Streptococcus bacteria because their shapes do not match. This specificity ensures that the immune response is precisely targeted against the invading pathogen without damaging host tissues. The lock-and-key interaction between the antibody's paratope (antigen-binding site) and the antigen's epitope is the basis of the specific immune response.

problems.final_answer: (a) Antibodies are Y-shaped proteins with two heavy and two light chains; they have variable regions at the tips (antigen-binding sites) and a constant region (Fc) that interacts with other immune components. (b) Antibodies neutralize toxins and viruses, opsonize pathogens for phagocytosis, activate complement proteins to lyse pathogens, and agglutinate pathogens. (c) Antibodies are specific because each antibody binds to a unique antigen epitope due to the complementary shape of its variable region.

problems.problem: (a) Define what is meant by an 'antibiotic.' (2 marks) (b) Explain why antibiotics are effective against bacteria but not against viruses. (2 marks) (c) Give one reason why it is important not to overuse antibiotics. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: An antibiotic is a substance (naturally produced by microorganisms, or synthetically manufactured) that kills or inhibits the growth of bacteria. Antibiotics are used to treat bacterial infections by targeting structures or processes that are essential for bacterial survival but are not present in human cells. The term 'antibiotic' originally referred to substances produced by one microorganism that kill another (e.g., penicillin from Penicillium mold), but it now includes synthetic antibacterial drugs. Antibiotics are classified by their mechanism of action: some are bactericidal (kill bacteria, e.g., penicillin, vancomycin), while others are bacteriostatic (inhibit bacterial growth, e.g., tetracycline, erythromycin).

step_by_step.step: Why antibiotics work against bacteria but not viruses (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Antibiotics are effective against bacteria but not against viruses because bacteria and viruses have fundamentally different biology: (1) Bacteria are living cells: They have their own metabolic machinery, ribosomes, cell walls, and replication systems. Antibiotics target these bacterial-specific structures and processes (e.g., penicillin inhibits cell wall synthesis; tetracycline inhibits bacterial ribosomes). (2) Viruses are not cells: Viruses lack their own metabolism, ribosomes, and replication machinery. They are obligate intracellular parasites that hijack host cell machinery to replicate. Since viruses use the host's cellular machinery (which is human in the case of human viruses), there are few virus-specific targets that can be attacked without harming the host cell. (3) Selective toxicity: Antibiotics rely on differences between bacterial and human cells. Viruses are so intimately integrated with host cells that it is difficult to develop drugs that kill the virus without killing the cell. Antiviral drugs (e.g., acyclovir for herpes, antiretrovirals for HIV) do exist, but they work by targeting virus-specific enzymes (e.g., viral polymerases, proteases) rather than the broader targets that antibiotics use.

step_by_step.step: Why overuse of antibiotics is harmful (part c)
step_by_step.explanation: Overuse of antibiotics is harmful primarily because it drives the evolution of antibiotic resistance. When antibiotics are used unnecessarily or incorrectly (e.g., not completing the full course), they exert selective pressure on bacterial populations. Bacteria that happen to carry resistance genes (either through mutation or horizontal gene transfer) survive and proliferate, while susceptible bacteria are eliminated. This leads to the emergence and spread of antibiotic-resistant bacteria (sometimes called 'superbugs'), such as methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB), and carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE). These resistant infections are much harder and more expensive to treat, and can be life-threatening. Additional harms include: disruption of the normal microbiome (leading to secondary infections like Clostridium difficile colitis), increased healthcare costs, and allergic reactions. The World Health Organization (WHO) identifies antimicrobial resistance as one of the top global public health threats.

problems.final_answer: (a) An antibiotic is a substance that kills or inhibits the growth of bacteria. (b) Antibiotics target bacterial-specific structures (cell walls, ribosomes) that are absent in viruses; viruses replicate inside host cells using host machinery, so antibiotics cannot affect them. (c) Overuse promotes antibiotic resistance in bacteria, making infections harder to treat.

problems.problem: (a) Explain how vaccination protects individuals against infectious disease. (4 marks) (b) Explain how vaccination can lead to herd immunity. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Vaccination works by artificially inducing immunological memory without causing the disease. The process is as follows: (1) Administration: A vaccine containing antigens (weakened live pathogens, inactivated (killed) pathogens, subunit antigens, or genetic material encoding antigens) is introduced into the body, typically by injection or oral administration. These antigens are recognized as foreign but are non-pathogenic (cannot cause disease). (2) Primary immune response: The vaccine antigens are taken up by antigen-presenting cells (macrophages, dendritic cells), which process them and present fragments on MHC molecules to naive B and T lymphocytes. This activates a primary immune response: B cells differentiate into plasma cells (producing antibodies) and memory B cells; T cells (both helper and cytotoxic) proliferate and differentiate into memory T cells. (3) Immunological memory: This primary response generates long-lived memory B cells and memory T cells that persist in the body for years or even decades. (4) Protection upon subsequent exposure: If the vaccinated individual later encounters the actual pathogen, the memory cells are rapidly activated. Memory B cells differentiate into plasma cells within days (compared to weeks for a primary response), producing large quantities of high-affinity antibodies. Memory T cells rapidly proliferate and mount a strong cell-mediated response. This accelerated and amplified secondary response eliminates the pathogen before it can cause significant illness. The individual is thus protected against the disease, though they may still experience mild or subclinical infection.

step_by_step.step: How vaccination leads to herd immunity (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Herd immunity (also called community immunity) occurs when a sufficiently high proportion of a population is immune to a disease (through vaccination or prior infection) that the spread of the pathogen is effectively halted. This protects the entire community, including those who cannot be vaccinated (e.g., infants too young for vaccines, immunocompromised individuals, people with vaccine allergies). The mechanism is: (1) Reduced transmission: When a high percentage of individuals are immune, the pathogen cannot easily find susceptible hosts. The basic reproduction number (R₀) of the disease—the average number of secondary cases from one infected individual—is reduced to below 1 (effective reproduction number R < 1). (2) Chain of transmission broken: Each infected individual encounters mostly immune individuals, so the chain of transmission is interrupted. The pathogen may die out in the population. (3) Threshold: The herd immunity threshold is calculated as 1 - (1/R₀). For highly contagious diseases like measles (R₀ = 12-18), approximately 92-95% of the population must be immune to achieve herd immunity. For less contagious diseases like influenza (R₀ ≈ 1.3-2.8), the threshold is lower (about 20-65%). Herd immunity is why high vaccination coverage is critical—it protects not only vaccinated individuals but also the most vulnerable members of the community.

problems.final_answer: (a) Vaccination introduces harmless antigens that trigger a primary immune response, generating memory B and T cells. Upon later exposure to the actual pathogen, these memory cells mount a rapid, strong secondary response that prevents disease. (b) Herd immunity occurs when a high proportion of the population is vaccinated, reducing pathogen transmission and protecting unvaccinated individuals because the pathogen cannot easily find susceptible hosts.

problems.problem: Distinguish between: (a) Active immunity and passive immunity (3 marks) (b) Natural immunity and artificial immunity (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Active immunity and passive immunity differ in several key aspects: (1) Source of antibodies/immune cells: Active immunity results from the individual's own immune system producing antibodies and memory cells in response to exposure to an antigen. Passive immunity results from receiving pre-formed antibodies or immune cells from another source (e.g., mother to baby, antibody injection). (2) Duration: Active immunity is long-lasting or permanent because memory cells persist in the body and can mount rapid responses upon re-exposure. Passive immunity is temporary, typically lasting weeks to months, as the transferred antibodies are gradually degraded and no memory cells are generated. (3) Onset: Active immunity has a delayed onset (days to weeks) because the immune system must mount a primary response. Passive immunity provides immediate protection because antibodies are already present. (4) Memory: Active immunity produces immunological memory; passive immunity does not. Examples of active immunity: recovering from chickenpox (natural active) or receiving the MMR vaccine (artificial active). Examples of passive immunity: maternal antibodies crossing the placenta (natural passive) or receiving rabies immunoglobulin after a bite (artificial passive).

Natural immunity and artificial immunity refer to how the immune protection is acquired, independent of whether it is active or passive: (1) Natural immunity: Acquired through normal life experiences without medical intervention. This includes: natural active immunity (recovering from an infection, e.g., having measles and then being immune) and natural passive immunity (antibodies transferred from mother to fetus via the placenta or through breast milk). (2) Artificial immunity: Acquired through deliberate medical intervention. This includes: artificial active immunity (vaccination, where antigens are intentionally introduced to stimulate the immune system) and artificial passive immunity (injection of pre-formed antibodies, such as antiserum or immunoglobulin, for immediate protection after exposure to a pathogen like rabies or tetanus). The distinction is therefore about the route of acquisition (natural vs. medical) rather than the mechanism of protection.

problems.final_answer: (a) Active immunity: body produces its own antibodies and memory cells; long-lasting; delayed onset. Passive immunity: receives pre-formed antibodies; temporary; immediate protection. (b) Natural immunity: acquired through normal life (infection or maternal transfer). Artificial immunity: acquired through medical intervention (vaccination or antibody injection).

problems.problem: (a) State what 'HIV' stands for. (1 mark) (b) Explain how HIV affects the immune system. (2 marks) (c) Describe two ways that HIV can be transmitted. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: HIV stands for Human Immunodeficiency Virus. 'Human' indicates it infects humans; 'Immunodeficiency' refers to the virus's effect of causing a deficiency in the immune system; 'Virus' identifies it as a viral pathogen.

step_by_step.step: How HIV affects the immune system (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: HIV primarily infects and destroys CD4⁺ T lymphocytes (also called helper T cells or Tₕ cells). These cells are central to the adaptive immune response, orchestrating both humoral (B cell) and cell-mediated (cytotoxic T cell) immunity. The mechanism: (1) Attachment and entry: HIV's surface glycoprotein (gp120) binds to the CD4 receptor on helper T cells and also requires a co-receptor (CCR5 or CXCR4) to enter the cell. (2) Replication: Once inside, HIV uses its reverse transcriptase enzyme to convert its RNA genome into DNA, which integrates into the host cell's genome. The infected cell then produces new viral particles, which bud out, destroying the host cell. (3) Progressive depletion: Over time (typically 8-10 years without treatment), the continuous destruction of CD4⁺ T cells leads to a gradual decline in their numbers. As CD4 counts fall below 200 cells/µL (normal is 500-1600), the immune system becomes severely compromised. (4) Consequences: Without sufficient helper T cells, the body cannot mount effective responses against opportunistic infections (infections by normally harmless pathogens) or certain cancers. This late stage of HIV infection is called AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome). The immune deficiency is not due to the virus itself directly killing all cells, but also to chronic immune activation, exhaustion, and destruction of lymphoid tissue architecture.

HIV is transmitted through direct contact with infected body fluids that contain sufficient viral load (high concentration of virus). The main transmission routes are: (1) Sexual contact: Unprotected vaginal, anal, or oral sex with an infected person. Anal sex carries the highest risk due to the thin, delicate mucosa and potential for microtears. (2) Blood-to-blood contact: Sharing contaminated needles or syringes (e.g., among people who inject drugs), accidental needlestick injuries in healthcare settings, transfusion of infected blood or blood products (rare in countries with screening), and tattoo or piercing equipment that is not properly sterilized. (3) Mother-to-child transmission (vertical transmission): During pregnancy (transplacentally), during childbirth (exposure to maternal blood and fluids), or through breastfeeding. With antiretroviral therapy (ART) during pregnancy and delivery, and avoiding breastfeeding in resource-rich settings, the risk can be reduced to less than 1%. HIV is NOT transmitted through casual contact such as hugging, shaking hands, sharing utensils, mosquito bites, or through air, saliva (unless blood is present), or sweat.

problems.final_answer: (a) HIV stands for Human Immunodeficiency Virus. (b) HIV infects and destroys helper T cells (CD4⁺ T cells), progressively weakening the immune system and leading to AIDS if untreated. (c) HIV can be transmitted through unprotected sexual contact and sharing contaminated needles (or mother-to-child during pregnancy/birth/breastfeeding).

problems.problem: (a) Describe how monoclonal antibodies are produced. (3 marks) (b) Give two uses of monoclonal antibodies in medicine. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are produced using hybridoma technology, which fuses antibody-producing B cells with immortal myeloma (cancer) cells. The process involves several steps: (1) Immunization: A mouse (or other mammal) is injected with a specific antigen to elicit an immune response. The mouse's B cells produce antibodies against that antigen. (2) Cell fusion: The mouse's spleen (containing B cells) is removed, and the B cells are fused with myeloma cells (cancerous B cells that can grow indefinitely in culture) using polyethylene glycol (PEG) or electrofusion. This creates hybridoma cells—hybrids that combine the antibody-producing capability of B cells with the immortality of myeloma cells. (3) Selection: The fused cells are grown in HAT (hypoxanthine-aminopterin-thymidine) medium, which kills unfused myeloma cells (which lack the HGPRT enzyme) while allowing hybridomas to survive. Unfused B cells naturally die after a few days. (4) Screening and cloning: The surviving hybridomas are screened to identify those that produce the desired antibody. Positive hybridomas are cloned (by limiting dilution) to establish a population of genetically identical cells, each producing the same specific antibody. (5) Production: The cloned hybridomas are cultured in large quantities (in bioreactors or in the peritoneal cavity of mice for ascites production) to produce large volumes of monoclonal antibodies, which are then harvested and purified. Modern techniques also use recombinant DNA technology to produce fully human monoclonal antibodies (using phage display or transgenic mice with human immune systems) to reduce the risk of human anti-mouse antibody (HAMA) reactions.

step_by_step.step: Uses of monoclonal antibodies in medicine (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Monoclonal antibodies have numerous medical applications: (1) Diagnostics: Pregnancy tests detect human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) using mAbs; rapid tests for infectious diseases (HIV, COVID-19, malaria) use mAbs to detect pathogen antigens or host antibodies; blood typing; cancer biomarker detection (e.g., HER2 in breast cancer); and immunohistochemistry for tissue diagnosis. (2) Therapeutics: Cancer treatment—mAbs can target cancer cells directly (e.g., rituximab for B-cell lymphomas targets CD20; trastuzumab for HER2-positive breast cancer blocks growth signals) or deliver chemotherapy/radiation specifically to cancer cells (antibody-drug conjugates, e.g., ado-trastuzumab emtansine). Autoimmune diseases—mAbs block inflammatory cytokines (e.g., adalimumab/Humira targeting TNF-α for rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn's disease). Infectious diseases—palivizumab prevents respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) in high-risk infants; some mAbs are used in Ebola treatment. Transplantation—mAbs (e.g., basiliximab) prevent organ rejection by blocking T cell activation. (3) Research: mAbs are essential tools in basic research for protein detection (Western blot), cell sorting (flow cytometry), and purification (affinity chromatography).

problems.final_answer: (a) Monoclonal antibodies are produced by fusing antibody-producing B cells from an immunized animal with immortal myeloma cells, creating hybridomas that are cloned to produce identical antibodies specific to one antigen. (b) Uses: diagnostic tests (e.g., pregnancy tests, COVID-19 rapid tests) and therapeutic treatments (e.g., cancer immunotherapy, autoimmune disease treatments).

problems.problem: Describe the signs of inflammation and explain how the inflammatory response helps to protect the body against infection.

step_by_step.explanation: The classical signs of inflammation are known by the Latin terms: rubor (redness), tumor (swelling), calor (heat), dolor (pain), and sometimes functio laesa (loss of function). These signs result from a coordinated vascular and cellular response to tissue injury or infection: (1) Redness (rubor): Caused by vasodilation of local blood vessels, increasing blood flow to the affected area. (2) Heat (calor): Also due to increased blood flow and increased metabolic activity in the inflamed tissue. (3) Swelling (tumor): Caused by increased vascular permeability, allowing fluid (exudate), proteins (including antibodies and complement), and immune cells to leak from blood vessels into the interstitial space (edema). (4) Pain (dolor): Caused by the swelling compressing nerve endings and by chemical mediators (bradykinin, prostaglandins, histamine) that sensitize or directly stimulate pain receptors. (5) Loss of function: May result from pain, swelling, or tissue damage, restricting movement to prevent further injury.

The inflammatory response is a protective mechanism that serves several critical functions: (1) Containment and isolation: Increased vascular permeability and fibrin deposition create a barrier that helps wall off the site of infection, preventing pathogens from spreading to other parts of the body. (2) Recruitment of immune cells: Chemokines and adhesion molecules (e.g., selectins, integrins) guide neutrophils, macrophages, and other immune cells from the bloodstream to the site of infection (chemotaxis). These cells then phagocytose and destroy pathogens. (3) Delivery of effector molecules: Increased blood flow and vascular permeability bring antibodies, complement proteins, and antimicrobial substances to the infection site. Complement activation enhances opsonization and lysis of pathogens. (4) Tissue repair: Inflammatory mediators also initiate the healing process by activating fibroblasts and promoting angiogenesis (formation of new blood vessels). (5) Initiation of adaptive immunity: Dendritic cells and macrophages activated during inflammation migrate to lymph nodes, where they present antigens to lymphocytes, initiating the specific adaptive immune response. (6) Systemic effects: Fever (caused by inflammatory cytokines like interleukin-1, TNF-α) enhances immune cell function and inhibits pathogen growth. While acute inflammation is protective, chronic inflammation (unresolved) can cause tissue damage and is associated with diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, and inflammatory bowel disease.

problems.final_answer: The signs of inflammation are redness, swelling, heat, pain, and loss of function. The inflammatory response protects by: increasing blood flow and vessel permeability to deliver immune cells and proteins to the infection site, isolating pathogens, recruiting phagocytes to destroy invaders, and initiating tissue repair.

problems.problem: Explain how each of the following helps to control the spread of infectious disease: (a) Improved sanitation (2 marks) (b) Safe water supplies (2 marks) (c) Vaccination programmes (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Improved sanitation controls the spread of infectious diseases by breaking the chain of transmission at the point of waste disposal. Sanitation refers to the safe management and disposal of human waste (feces and urine). (1) Prevention of fecal-oral transmission: Many pathogens (e.g., cholera, typhoid, polio, hepatitis A, norovirus, rotavirus) are transmitted through the fecal-oral route—pathogens from infected individuals contaminate the environment and are ingested by new hosts. Proper sanitation systems (sewage treatment, septic tanks, latrines) ensure that feces are contained and treated, preventing contamination of soil, water sources, and food. (2) Reduction of vector breeding sites: Poor sanitation (open defecation, stagnant sewage) creates breeding grounds for disease vectors such as flies (which mechanically transmit pathogens) and mosquitoes (e.g., Culex mosquitoes that transmit West Nile virus). Improved sanitation eliminates these habitats. (3) Dignity and hygiene: Sanitation facilities enable handwashing and improve overall hygiene practices. The WHO estimates that inadequate sanitation contributes to approximately 500,000 deaths annually from diarrheal diseases, many of which are children under five.

Safe water supplies control infectious disease by preventing waterborne and water-washed infections. (1) Prevention of waterborne diseases: Many pathogens are transmitted through contaminated drinking water, including bacteria (Vibrio cholerae, Salmonella typhi, Campylobacter), viruses (norovirus, hepatitis A, rotavirus), and protozoa (Giardia, Cryptosporidium, Entamoeba histolytica). Safe water supplies—through treatment (filtration, chlorination, UV disinfection), protected sources (boreholes, capped wells), and proper distribution systems—eliminate or inactivate these pathogens, preventing ingestion. (2) Hygiene promotion: Access to safe, reliable water enables handwashing with soap, which is one of the most effective interventions for preventing diarrheal diseases and respiratory infections (including COVID-19). The WHO estimates that handwashing can reduce diarrheal disease risk by up to 47% and respiratory infections by up to 23%. (3) Reduction of water-washed diseases: Diseases like scabies and trachoma (a leading cause of blindness) are associated with inadequate water for bathing and washing; safe water supplies reduce their transmission. (4) Food safety: Safe water is essential for washing food and for food preparation, reducing the risk of foodborne illness.

Vaccination programmes control the spread of infectious diseases through multiple mechanisms: (1) Individual protection: Vaccinated individuals are protected from disease and, in many cases, from infection entirely (sterilizing immunity). They do not become sick and often cannot transmit the pathogen to others. (2) Herd immunity: When vaccination coverage reaches a sufficiently high threshold (e.g., 95% for measles), transmission is interrupted because the pathogen cannot find enough susceptible hosts to sustain chains of infection. This protects vulnerable individuals who cannot be vaccinated (infants, immunocompromised, those with contraindications). (3) Eradication and elimination: High-coverage vaccination programmes have led to the eradication of smallpox (declared eradicated by WHO in 1980) and the near-eradication of polio (wild poliovirus remains in only two countries as of 2024). Measles elimination has been achieved in many regions. (4) Reduced disease burden: Routine childhood vaccination programmes have dramatically reduced mortality and morbidity from vaccine-preventable diseases. The WHO estimates that vaccines prevent 3.5-5 million deaths annually from diseases like diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, measles, and influenza. (5) Cost-effectiveness: Vaccination is one of the most cost-effective public health interventions, preventing not only illness but also healthcare costs and productivity losses associated with outbreaks.

problems.final_answer: (a) Improved sanitation prevents fecal-oral transmission by safely disposing of human waste, reducing contamination of the environment. (b) Safe water supplies prevent waterborne diseases by eliminating pathogens from drinking water and enabling handwashing. (c) Vaccination programmes protect individuals and create herd immunity, reducing pathogen circulation and preventing outbreaks.

problems.problem: (a) Explain why viruses are described as 'obligate intracellular parasites.' (2 marks) (b) Describe how viruses replicate inside host cells. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Viruses are described as obligate intracellular parasites because they cannot replicate (reproduce) outside a living host cell. Unlike cellular organisms (bacteria, fungi, protozoa), viruses lack: (1) Their own metabolic machinery: They have no ribosomes, no ATP-generating systems, and no enzymes for protein synthesis or energy production. (2) The ability to replicate independently: Viruses carry only the genetic material (DNA or RNA) and, in some cases, a few enzymes (e.g., reverse transcriptase, RNA polymerase). They must hijack a host cell's ribosomes, enzymes, nucleotides, and energy to synthesize viral proteins and replicate their genome. (3) Cellular structure: Viruses are not cells; they consist of a nucleic acid core surrounded by a protein capsid (and sometimes an envelope). Outside a host cell, viruses are metabolically inert and cannot carry out any life processes. They are essentially 'biological packages' that become active only after entering a suitable host cell. This obligate intracellular nature distinguishes viruses from other pathogens.

step_by_step.step: Viral replication inside host cells (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Viral replication follows a general sequence of steps, though details vary by virus type (DNA vs. RNA, enveloped vs. non-enveloped): (1) Attachment and entry: The virus binds to specific receptor molecules on the host cell surface (e.g., HIV binds to CD4 and CCR5/CXCR4; influenza virus binds to sialic acid). The virus then enters the cell through endocytosis or, for enveloped viruses, fusion with the cell membrane. (2) Uncoating: The viral capsid is disassembled, releasing the viral genome (DNA or RNA) into the host cell's cytoplasm or nucleus. (3) Replication and transcription: The viral genome is replicated to produce many copies. Depending on the virus type: DNA viruses typically use host cell DNA polymerases to replicate in the nucleus; RNA viruses use viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerases (replicases) in the cytoplasm; retroviruses (like HIV) use reverse transcriptase to convert RNA into DNA, which integrates into the host genome. Viral genes are transcribed to produce messenger RNA (mRNA) for protein synthesis. (4) Translation and protein synthesis: Host cell ribosomes translate viral mRNA into viral proteins: structural proteins (capsid components, envelope proteins) and non-structural proteins (enzymes needed for replication). (5) Assembly: New viral genomes and capsid proteins self-assemble into complete virions (virus particles). For enveloped viruses, assembly occurs at the cell membrane, where the virus acquires its envelope by budding. (6) Release: New virions are released from the host cell, either by lysis (rupture of the cell, killing it) or by budding (which may or may not kill the cell immediately). These new virions go on to infect other cells, perpetuating the infection.

problems.final_answer: (a) Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites because they lack their own metabolic machinery and cannot replicate outside a living host cell; they must use host cell ribosomes, enzymes, and energy to reproduce. (b) Viruses replicate by: attaching to host cells and entering; uncoating to release their genome; using host machinery to replicate their genome and synthesize viral proteins; assembling new virus particles; and releasing them to infect other cells.

problems.problem: (a) Name the pathogen that causes malaria. (1 mark) (b) Name the vector that transmits malaria. (1 mark) (c) Explain why malaria is more common in tropical regions. (2 marks) (d) Describe one method of preventing malaria. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Malaria is caused by protozoan parasites of the genus Plasmodium. Four species commonly infect humans: Plasmodium falciparum (the most deadly, responsible for most malaria deaths), Plasmodium vivax (the most widespread), Plasmodium ovale, and Plasmodium malariae. A fifth species, Plasmodium knowlesi, is a zoonotic parasite that also infects humans in Southeast Asia. The parasites are transmitted through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.

The vector that transmits malaria is the female Anopheles mosquito. Only female Anopheles mosquitoes transmit malaria because they require a blood meal to develop their eggs. Male mosquitoes do not feed on blood and are not involved in transmission. Different Anopheles species serve as vectors in different regions (e.g., Anopheles gambiae complex in Africa, Anopheles stephensi in Asia). The mosquito becomes infected when it takes a blood meal from an infected human, ingesting Plasmodium gametocytes, which undergo sexual reproduction and development within the mosquito before migrating to the salivary glands for transmission to the next human host.

step_by_step.step: Why malaria is more common in tropical regions (part c)
step_by_step.explanation: Malaria is more common in tropical and subtropical regions due to a combination of environmental and ecological factors that favor both the mosquito vector and the Plasmodium parasite: (1) Temperature: The Plasmodium parasite requires a minimum temperature of approximately 16-20°C to complete its development (sporogony) inside the mosquito. Higher temperatures (20-30°C) accelerate parasite development and shorten the extrinsic incubation period, increasing transmission efficiency. Tropical regions maintain these temperatures year-round. (2) Rainfall and humidity: Mosquitoes require standing water (pools, puddles, rice paddies) for breeding. Tropical regions have abundant rainfall and high humidity, providing ideal breeding habitats. (3) Vector longevity: Anopheles mosquitoes live longer in tropical climates, increasing the probability that they will survive the 10-14 day period required for parasite development and subsequently transmit the disease through multiple blood meals. (4) Lack of seasonal interruption: In temperate regions, cold winters interrupt mosquito breeding and parasite development, eliminating or dramatically reducing transmission seasonally. In the tropics, transmission can occur year-round. (5) Socioeconomic factors: Many tropical regions have limited resources for vector control, mosquito net distribution, and healthcare infrastructure, contributing to higher disease burden.

Malaria prevention includes several strategies: (1) Vector control: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs) or long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs) to prevent mosquito bites during sleep (when Anopheles mosquitoes are most active). Indoor residual spraying (IRS) with insecticides kills mosquitoes resting on walls. (2) Chemoprophylaxis: Antimalarial drugs (e.g., doxycycline, atovaquone-proguanil, mefloquine) taken before, during, and after travel to endemic areas to prevent infection. (3) Larval source management: Elimination of standing water where mosquitoes breed. (4) Personal protection: Wearing long-sleeved clothing, using mosquito repellents (DEET, picaridin), and sleeping in screened or air-conditioned rooms. (5) Vaccination: The RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix) vaccine is the first approved malaria vaccine, recommended for children in endemic regions. It provides partial protection and is used alongside other preventive measures.

problems.final_answer: (a) Plasmodium (a protozoan). (b) Female Anopheles mosquito. (c) Tropical regions have year-round warm temperatures (required for parasite development in mosquitoes) and abundant rainfall (providing breeding sites). (d) Prevention: use of insecticide-treated bed nets (or indoor residual spraying, antimalarial drugs, or elimination of standing water).

problems.problem: Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial disease that primarily affects the lungs. (a) Explain how TB is transmitted from person to person. (2 marks) (b) Describe two symptoms of TB. (2 marks) (c) Explain why people with weakened immune systems are more susceptible to TB. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Tuberculosis is transmitted through airborne droplets (aerosol transmission). When a person with active pulmonary or laryngeal TB coughs, sneezes, talks, or sings, they expel tiny droplets (aerosols) containing the bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis). These droplets (1-5 micrometers in diameter) can remain suspended in the air for several hours, especially in poorly ventilated, enclosed spaces. A susceptible person inhales these droplets, and the bacteria travel to the alveoli (air sacs) of the lungs, where they are taken up by alveolar macrophages and establish infection. Unlike many other pathogens, TB does not spread through casual contact (shaking hands, sharing utensils) or by contact with surfaces—it requires inhalation of infectious droplets. Prolonged close contact (e.g., household members, crowded living conditions) is typically required for transmission.

The symptoms of active tuberculosis are primarily pulmonary but can also be systemic. Common symptoms include: (1) Persistent cough: A chronic cough lasting three weeks or longer, often producing sputum (phlegm) that may be blood-stained (hemoptysis) due to tissue damage in the lungs. The cough may be productive (mucus or pus) or dry. (2) Chest pain: Pain in the chest, especially with coughing or deep breathing, resulting from inflammation of the pleura (pleurisy) or from lung tissue damage. (3) Systemic symptoms: Unexplained weight loss (often significant), night sweats (drenching sweats during sleep), fever (typically low-grade, persistent, and often occurring in the afternoon/evening), fatigue, and loss of appetite. (4) In extrapulmonary TB (when the infection spreads beyond the lungs): symptoms depend on the affected site, such as lymphadenopathy (swollen lymph nodes, often in the neck), back pain (spinal TB/Pott's disease), or headache and confusion (TB meningitis).

step_by_step.step: Why weakened immunity increases susceptibility (part c)
step_by_step.explanation: People with weakened immune systems are more susceptible to TB because the immune system normally contains the infection in a state called latent TB infection (LTBI). In healthy individuals, after initial infection, macrophages and T cells form granulomas—organized structures that wall off the bacteria, keeping them dormant but alive. The bacteria can remain in this latent state for years or decades without causing symptoms. However, when the immune system is compromised—due to HIV/AIDS (the strongest risk factor), immunosuppressive medications (e.g., for organ transplant, autoimmune diseases), malnutrition, diabetes, or chemotherapy—the immune system can no longer maintain these granulomas. The bacteria escape containment, proliferate, and cause active TB disease. HIV and TB have a synergistic relationship: HIV increases the risk of latent TB reactivating to active disease by 20-30 times, and TB accelerates HIV progression. This is why TB is the leading cause of death among people living with HIV.

problems.final_answer: (a) TB is transmitted through airborne droplets: infected individuals cough or sneeze, releasing bacteria-laden droplets that are inhaled by others. (b) Symptoms include persistent cough (sometimes with blood), chest pain, weight loss, night sweats, and fever. (c) Weakened immune systems cannot maintain latent TB containment, allowing bacteria to reactivate and cause active disease.

problems.problem: (a) Name two bacteria commonly associated with food poisoning. (2 marks) (b) Explain two ways to prevent food poisoning in the home. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Common bacteria that cause food poisoning include: (1) Salmonella species (e.g., Salmonella enterica, Salmonella typhimurium): Found in raw or undercooked eggs, poultry, meat, and unpasteurized milk. Causes symptoms of nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fever, and abdominal cramps 6-72 hours after ingestion. (2) Campylobacter jejuni: One of the most common causes of bacterial gastroenteritis worldwide; associated with undercooked poultry, unpasteurized milk, and contaminated water. Causes severe diarrhea (often bloody), fever, and abdominal pain. (3) Escherichia coli (especially pathogenic strains like E. coli O157:H7): Found in undercooked ground beef, contaminated produce, and unpasteurized milk. Produces Shiga toxin causing severe bloody diarrhea and potentially hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS), a serious kidney complication. (4) Staphylococcus aureus: Produces heat-stable toxins in improperly stored foods (meat, dairy, pastries). Symptoms (nausea, vomiting, cramping) appear rapidly (1-6 hours) after ingestion. (5) Clostridium perfringens: Often associated with large-scale food preparation (cafeterias, catering) where food is kept at unsafe temperatures; causes abdominal cramps and diarrhea. (6) Listeria monocytogenes: Found in ready-to-eat foods (deli meats, soft cheeses, unpasteurized dairy); particularly dangerous for pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised individuals.

step_by_step.step: Prevention of food poisoning in the home (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Food poisoning can be prevented through proper food handling practices following the 'Four C's' (Clean, Cook, Chill, Cross-contamination prevention): (1) Cook food thoroughly: Ensure that foods (especially poultry, meat, eggs, and seafood) are cooked to safe internal temperatures (e.g., poultry to 74°C/165°F, ground meat to 71°C/160°F) to kill harmful bacteria. Use a food thermometer rather than relying on color. Avoid eating raw or undercooked eggs, meat, or unpasteurized dairy products. (2) Prevent cross-contamination: Use separate cutting boards, utensils, and plates for raw meat/poultry and ready-to-eat foods (vegetables, cooked foods). Wash hands, surfaces, and utensils with hot soapy water after handling raw meat. Never place cooked food on a plate that previously held raw meat. (3) Proper storage: Refrigerate perishable foods promptly (within 2 hours, or 1 hour if ambient temperature is above 32°C/90°F). Keep refrigerator temperature below 4°C (40°F) and freezer below -18°C (0°F). Thaw frozen foods in the refrigerator, cold water, or microwave—never on the counter. (4) Wash hands and surfaces: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before and after handling food, after using the bathroom, and after touching pets. Wash fruits and vegetables under running water. (5) Avoid the 'danger zone': Do not leave perishable food at room temperature for more than 2 hours; bacteria grow most rapidly between 4°C and 60°C (40°F-140°F).

problems.final_answer: (a) Salmonella and Campylobacter (or E. coli, Staphylococcus aureus). (b) Prevent food poisoning by: cooking food thoroughly (especially meat and poultry) to kill bacteria; preventing cross-contamination by using separate cutting boards for raw meat and ready-to-eat foods; and refrigerating perishable foods promptly.

problems.problem: Explain why a person who has had measles is unlikely to catch the disease again. Refer to memory cells in your answer.

step_by_step.explanation: A person who has had measles is unlikely to catch the disease again because the initial infection generates immunological memory, providing lifelong immunity. When the measles virus first enters the body, it triggers a primary immune response. B lymphocytes specific to measles antigens are activated (with help from helper T cells) and differentiate into plasma cells that produce antibodies to neutralize the virus, and memory B cells that persist long-term. Simultaneously, cytotoxic T cells (CD8⁺) eliminate virus-infected cells, and memory T cells are generated. After the infection is cleared, most of the plasma cells die, but the memory B cells and memory T cells remain in the body for decades, often for life.

If the same person is exposed to the measles virus again (e.g., decades later), the memory cells mount a rapid and amplified secondary immune response. Memory B cells, which have undergone affinity maturation (producing antibodies with higher binding affinity), quickly differentiate into plasma cells that secrete large quantities of high-affinity anti-measles antibodies. These antibodies neutralize the virus before it can establish infection. Memory T cells (both helper and cytotoxic) rapidly proliferate, providing support for B cell activation and eliminating any infected cells. This secondary response is so rapid and effective that the virus is cleared before symptoms develop. This is why natural infection with measles (or other viruses like mumps, rubella, chickenpox) typically confers lifelong immunity. The same principle underlies vaccination: inactivated or attenuated measles vaccine generates memory cells without causing disease, providing protection without the risks of natural infection (which can include pneumonia, encephalitis, and death).

problems.final_answer: After measles infection, memory B cells and memory T cells remain in the body. Upon re-exposure, these memory cells rapidly produce high-affinity antibodies and mount a strong cellular response, eliminating the virus before symptoms develop, providing lifelong immunity.

problems.problem: (a) Define what is meant by an 'epidemic.' (2 marks) (b) Explain why influenza is able to cause pandemics. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: An epidemic is the occurrence of cases of a disease in a population or region that is clearly in excess of what is normally expected (the endemic level). Epidemics are characterized by: (1) Sudden increase: A rapid rise in the number of cases above baseline levels. (2) Time-limited: Epidemics have a defined beginning and end, though the duration varies. (3) Geographic scope: Typically confined to a specific community, region, or country. When an epidemic spreads across multiple countries or continents and affects a large number of people, it becomes a pandemic. When a disease is consistently present at low levels in a population, it is endemic (e.g., malaria in parts of Africa). An outbreak is often used interchangeably with epidemic but may refer to a more localized increase (e.g., a foodborne outbreak in a single town).

Influenza is able to cause pandemics due to several characteristics of the virus: (1) Antigenic shift (major genetic change): Influenza A viruses have segmented genomes (8 RNA segments). When two different influenza viruses infect the same cell (e.g., an avian influenza virus and a human influenza virus), they can reassort, swapping entire gene segments to produce a novel subtype. This is called antigenic shift. If the new virus is antigenically distinct from circulating strains (e.g., a novel hemagglutinin (H) or neuraminidase (N) subtype to which the human population has no pre-existing immunity), it can cause a pandemic. Major pandemics (1918 H1N1 Spanish flu, 1957 H2N2 Asian flu, 1968 H3N2 Hong Kong flu, 2009 H1N1 pandemic) resulted from such shifts. (2) Antigenic drift (continuous change): Influenza viruses also undergo frequent, small mutations (antigenic drift) that allow them to evade existing immunity, causing seasonal epidemics. (3) Respiratory transmission: Influenza is transmitted via airborne droplets and aerosols, allowing rapid spread in populations. (4) Short incubation period (1-4 days) and period of contagiousness before symptoms appear (pre-symptomatic transmission) facilitate rapid dissemination. (5) Global travel: Modern air travel enables a novel influenza virus to spread globally within weeks. (6) Animal reservoirs: Influenza A viruses have reservoirs in wild aquatic birds and can also infect pigs, which can serve as 'mixing vessels' for reassortment. The 2009 H1N1 pandemic virus, for example, contained gene segments from human, avian, and swine influenza viruses.

problems.final_answer: (a) An epidemic is a sudden increase in the number of cases of a disease above what is normally expected in a specific population or region. (b) Influenza causes pandemics because it undergoes antigenic shift (major genetic reassortment creating novel subtypes with no pre-existing immunity) and is transmitted rapidly via airborne droplets, spreading globally through travel.

problems.problem: (a) Describe two physical defences in plants. (2 marks) (b) Describe one chemical defence in plants. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Plants have several physical barriers that prevent pathogen entry and establishment: (1) Bark and cuticle: The outer bark of woody plants and the waxy cuticle covering leaves and stems provide a physical barrier that prevents pathogen penetration. The cuticle is composed of cutin (a hydrophobic polymer) and waxes, making it difficult for bacteria and fungal hyphae to breach. Thick bark protects against mechanical damage and fire, and also contains cork cells that are impregnated with suberin, which is resistant to microbial degradation. (2) Cell wall: Plant cells have rigid cell walls made primarily of cellulose, hemicellulose, and pectin, which form a physical barrier. The middle lamella (rich in pectin) holds cells together; pathogens must produce enzymes (cutinases, pectinases, cellulases) to break through, which triggers plant defence responses. (3) Trichomes: Leaf hairs (trichomes) can act as a physical barrier, trapping insects and creating a microclimate less favorable for pathogen germination. Some trichomes are glandular and produce chemical deterrents. (4) Stomatal closure: Plants can close stomata in response to pathogen detection (via pattern recognition receptors), physically blocking entry points that bacteria (like Pseudomonas syringae) use to enter leaves. (5) Casparian strip: In roots, the Casparian strip (a suberin-containing band in endodermal cells) blocks apoplastic flow, preventing pathogens in the soil from moving into the vascular tissue.

Plants produce a wide array of chemical compounds that inhibit pathogen growth or deter herbivores. Key chemical defences include: (1) Phytoalexins: These are antimicrobial compounds synthesized de novo in response to pathogen attack. Examples include camalexin in Arabidopsis, pisatin in peas, and resveratrol in grapes. Phytoalexins are toxic to fungi, bacteria, and nematodes, and accumulate at the site of infection. (2) Antimicrobial proteins: Plants produce pathogenesis-related (PR) proteins, including chitinases (which degrade fungal cell walls), glucanases (which break down fungal β-glucans), and defensins (small cysteine-rich peptides that disrupt microbial membranes). (3) Preformed antimicrobial compounds: Some plants constitutively produce antimicrobial substances. For example, tannins in bark and leaves have antimicrobial properties; alkaloids (e.g., nicotine in tobacco, caffeine in coffee) are toxic to insects and microbes; and phenolic compounds (e.g., salicylic acid) are involved in systemic acquired resistance (SAR) signaling. (4) Hypersensitive response (HR): While not a chemical per se, HR involves localized programmed cell death at the infection site, isolating pathogens and releasing antimicrobial compounds. (5) Volatile organic compounds (VOCs): Plants can emit volatile compounds that attract predators of herbivores or signal neighboring plants to activate defences.

problems.final_answer: (a) Physical defences: bark and waxy cuticle (prevent pathogen entry), and cell walls (physical barrier). (b) Chemical defence: phytoalexins—antimicrobial compounds produced in response to infection—or antimicrobial proteins like chitinases that degrade fungal cell walls.

problems.problem: (a) Explain the process of pasteurisation. (2 marks) (b) Distinguish between pasteurisation and sterilisation. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Pasteurisation is a heat treatment process that reduces the number of pathogenic microorganisms in food (particularly milk and other beverages) to a level that does not cause disease, while minimizing changes to the food's nutritional value and sensory qualities. The process involves heating the liquid to a specific temperature for a defined time and then rapidly cooling it. Common methods include: (1) High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST): Heating to 72°C (161°F) for 15 seconds, followed by rapid cooling. This is the most common method for milk pasteurisation. (2) Low-Temperature Long-Time (LTLT): Heating to 63°C (145°F) for 30 minutes. (3) Ultra-High Temperature (UHT): Heating to 135-150°C (275-302°F) for 1-2 seconds, producing shelf-stable (aseptic) products that do not require refrigeration until opened. Pasteurisation targets vegetative bacterial pathogens such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis (TB), Salmonella, Listeria, Campylobacter, and Escherichia coli, as well as spoilage organisms. It does not kill all microorganisms—spore-forming bacteria (e.g., Bacillus, Clostridium) survive—which is why pasteurised products still require refrigeration and have a limited shelf life.

Pasteurisation and sterilisation differ in their objectives, methods, and outcomes: (1) Objective: Pasteurisation aims to reduce pathogenic microorganisms to safe levels while preserving food quality; it does not eliminate all microorganisms. Sterilisation aims to eliminate all microorganisms, including bacterial endospores, achieving complete microbial destruction. (2) Temperature and time: Pasteurisation uses relatively mild heat (typically 63-72°C) for minutes to seconds. Sterilisation uses more intense heat (typically 121°C under pressure (autoclaving) for 15-20 minutes, or dry heat at higher temperatures for longer periods) to kill spores. (3) Outcome: Pasteurised products are not sterile; they contain non-pathogenic bacteria and bacterial spores and must be refrigerated. Sterilised products are commercially sterile—they contain no viable microorganisms or spores and can be stored at room temperature indefinitely if the container remains sealed. (4) Applications: Pasteurisation is used for milk, juice, beer, and eggs. Sterilisation is used for surgical instruments (autoclaving), canned foods (retort processing), and medical supplies. (5) Effect on food: Pasteurisation minimally affects flavor, texture, and nutrients. Sterilisation (especially thermal) can significantly alter taste, texture, and nutritional content.

problems.final_answer: (a) Pasteurisation is heating a liquid (e.g., milk) to a specific temperature (e.g., 72°C for 15 seconds) to kill pathogenic bacteria without affecting taste or nutritional value. (b) Pasteurisation reduces pathogens but does not kill all microbes; sterilisation eliminates all microorganisms (including spores) completely, typically using higher temperatures or pressure.

problems.problem: (a) Describe how tears protect the eyes from infection. (2 marks) (b) Explain how the skin produces a hostile environment for bacteria. (2 marks) (c) Name the substance found in sweat that inhibits bacterial growth. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Tears protect the eyes from infection through multiple mechanisms: (1) Mechanical flushing: The constant flow of tears across the surface of the eye (produced by lacrimal glands) physically washes away foreign particles, dust, and microorganisms, preventing them from adhering to the conjunctiva and cornea. Blinking distributes tears and helps remove debris. (2) Antimicrobial components: Tears contain lysozyme, an enzyme that hydrolyzes the peptidoglycan layer of bacterial cell walls, particularly effective against Gram-positive bacteria (e.g., Staphylococcus, Streptococcus). Lysozyme cleaves the β-1,4 glycosidic bonds between N-acetylmuramic acid and N-acetylglucosamine in peptidoglycan, causing bacterial lysis. (3) Additional antimicrobial substances: Tears also contain lactoferrin (an iron-binding protein that sequesters iron essential for bacterial growth), lipocalin (which binds bacterial siderophores), secretory IgA (antibodies that bind pathogens), and defensins (antimicrobial peptides). (4) Reflex tearing: Irritation triggers increased tear production, enhancing the protective response. Together, these mechanisms make the ocular surface inhospitable to most pathogens, though some (e.g., Chlamydia trachomatis causing trachoma) have evolved to evade these defences.

The skin creates a hostile environment for bacteria through several chemical and physical factors: (1) Low pH (acid mantle): The skin surface is acidic, with a pH typically between 4.5 and 5.5. This acidity is maintained by sweat (containing lactic acid, urea, and amino acids) and sebum (containing fatty acids). The low pH inhibits the growth of many pathogenic bacteria that prefer neutral pH (7.0-7.4). The acid mantle also supports the growth of commensal bacteria that outcompete pathogens. (2) Sebum: Sebaceous glands secrete sebum, an oily mixture of triglycerides, wax esters, squalene, and fatty acids. Sebum has antimicrobial properties; fatty acids (particularly lauric acid and sapienic acid) are toxic to many bacteria and fungi. Sebum also creates a hydrophobic barrier that prevents water loss and inhibits the growth of moisture-loving pathogens. (3) Salt: Sweat contains high concentrations of sodium chloride (salt), creating a hypertonic environment that can dehydrate and inhibit salt-sensitive bacteria. (4) Antimicrobial peptides: Skin produces antimicrobial peptides such as defensins (β-defensins), cathelicidin (LL-37), and dermcidin. Dermcidin, secreted by sweat glands, is active against a broad range of bacteria, including Staphylococcus aureus. (5) Desiccation: The outer layer of the skin (stratum corneum) is relatively dry, which inhibits bacterial growth (most bacteria require high moisture). (6) Continuous shedding: The constant shedding of dead skin cells removes bacteria that have colonized the surface.

The substance found in sweat that inhibits bacterial growth is dermcidin. Dermcidin is an antimicrobial peptide secreted by eccrine sweat glands that is active against a broad range of bacteria, including Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Candida albicans. Unlike many antimicrobial peptides that disrupt bacterial membranes, dermcidin forms ion channels in bacterial membranes, leading to membrane depolarization and bacterial death. It is active over a wide pH range and remains functional even in the high-salt environment of sweat. Other sweat components with antimicrobial activity include lysozyme (also present in tears), lactoferrin, and IgA, but dermcidin is unique to sweat and is the primary sweat-derived antimicrobial factor.

problems.final_answer: (a) Tears physically flush away pathogens and contain lysozyme (enzyme that breaks down bacterial cell walls). (b) Skin is acidic (low pH), produces antimicrobial sebum, and has a dry, salty environment that inhibits bacterial growth. (c) Dermcidin (or lysozyme).

problems.problem: Explain why new infectious diseases continue to emerge and spread. Give two reasons.

step_by_step.step: Reason 1: Zoonotic spillover and environmental change
step_by_step.explanation: New infectious diseases continue to emerge primarily because of zoonotic spillover—the transmission of pathogens from animals to humans. Approximately 60-75% of emerging infectious diseases are zoonotic in origin. Factors driving this include: (1) Habitat destruction and deforestation: As human populations expand into previously undisturbed ecosystems, contact between humans and wildlife increases. Deforestation for agriculture, mining, and urban development brings humans into closer proximity with reservoir species (e.g., bats, primates, rodents). For example, the Nipah virus emerged in Malaysia and Bangladesh when pig farms encroached into bat habitat; Ebola outbreaks are linked to forest encroachment; and SARS-CoV-2 likely originated in bats with potential intermediate hosts. (2) Wildlife trade and consumption: The commercial trade in wild animals (live animal markets, bushmeat) creates conditions for cross-species transmission. Wet markets where multiple animal species are housed in close proximity facilitate viral reassortment and spillover. (3) Agricultural intensification: High-density livestock farming creates ideal conditions for pathogen amplification and evolution. Influenza viruses (avian and swine) reassort in intensively farmed animals, producing novel strains that can jump to humans (e.g., H5N1, H7N9). (4) Climate change: Altered temperature and precipitation patterns are shifting the geographic ranges of vectors (mosquitoes, ticks) and reservoir species, introducing pathogens to previously unexposed populations. Dengue, chikungunya, and Zika have expanded into temperate regions as Aedes mosquito ranges expand.

step_by_step.step: Reason 2: Global travel, urbanization, and population density
step_by_step.explanation: Modern interconnectedness enables emerging pathogens to spread globally at unprecedented speed: (1) Air travel: International travel allows a pathogen to move from a remote location to major cities worldwide within 24-48 hours. The 2003 SARS outbreak spread from Hong Kong to Toronto, Singapore, and Vietnam within days; the 2009 H1N1 pandemic spread to over 200 countries within months; and COVID-19 spread globally in weeks. (2) Urbanization and population density: Over 55% of the world's population now lives in cities, with many megacities in low- and middle-income countries having high population densities, inadequate sanitation, and limited healthcare infrastructure. Dense urban populations facilitate rapid person-to-person transmission of respiratory viruses and waterborne pathogens. (3) International trade: Global food supply chains can disseminate pathogens across continents (e.g., contaminated produce causing multi-country Salmonella outbreaks). (4) Antimicrobial resistance: Overuse and misuse of antibiotics in humans and agriculture have driven the emergence of antimicrobial-resistant bacteria (e.g., MRSA, carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae), creating 'new' pathogens that are difficult to treat. (5) Increased immunocompromised populations: An aging global population, increased use of immunosuppressive therapies, and the HIV pandemic have expanded the pool of vulnerable hosts, allowing opportunistic pathogens to emerge. (6) Social and behavioral factors: Changes in sexual behavior (HIV emergence), injection drug use (HCV, HIV), and cultural practices (burial practices in Ebola outbreaks) influence pathogen transmission. (7) Healthcare-associated transmission: Inadequate infection control in healthcare settings can amplify emerging pathogens (e.g., MDR-TB outbreaks, Ebola transmission to healthcare workers).

problems.final_answer: New infectious diseases emerge due to: (1) Zoonotic spillover—increased human contact with wildlife through deforestation, habitat destruction, and wildlife trade allows animal pathogens to jump to humans. (2) Global travel and urbanization—rapid international travel and dense urban populations enable rapid spread of emerging pathogens worldwide.

problems.problem: Describe the difference between: (a) Live attenuated vaccines (2 marks) (b) Inactivated (killed) vaccines (2 marks) (c) Give one advantage and one disadvantage of live attenuated vaccines. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Live attenuated vaccines contain weakened (attenuated) versions of the live pathogen that can still replicate in the host but do not cause disease in healthy individuals. Attenuation is achieved by growing the pathogen in conditions that select for reduced virulence (e.g., culturing at suboptimal temperatures, repeated passage in cell culture, or genetic engineering). Examples include the measles, mumps, rubella (MMR) vaccine, varicella (chickenpox) vaccine, rotavirus vaccine, yellow fever vaccine, and BCG (tuberculosis) vaccine. Because the vaccine contains live replicating organisms, it elicits a strong and long-lasting immune response that closely mimics natural infection, typically including both humoral (antibody) and cell-mediated (T cell) immunity. A single dose often provides lifelong immunity. However, live vaccines are contraindicated in immunocompromised individuals because even the attenuated pathogen could cause disease. They also require careful handling and refrigeration to maintain viability.

Inactivated vaccines contain whole pathogens that have been killed (inactivated) by heat, chemicals (formaldehyde), or radiation. Because they cannot replicate, they are safer for immunocompromised individuals and more stable (less temperature-sensitive) than live vaccines. Examples include the inactivated polio vaccine (IPV), hepatitis A vaccine, influenza vaccine (injectable form), rabies vaccine, and whole-cell pertussis vaccine (included in DTaP). Since inactivated vaccines do not replicate, they typically elicit a weaker immune response, primarily humoral (antibody-mediated) with little to no cell-mediated immunity. Multiple doses (booster shots) are usually required to achieve and maintain immunity. Inactivated vaccines often require adjuvants (e.g., aluminum salts) to enhance the immune response. Some inactivated vaccines may also be subunit vaccines (containing only specific antigens rather than the whole pathogen) or conjugate vaccines (where a polysaccharide antigen is linked to a protein carrier to improve immune response in infants).
